questions.question: Explain the purpose of sigprocmask() function in UNIX/Linux system programming.
keyPoints: sigprocmask() examines and modifies the signal mask
The signal mask determines which signals are blocked from delivery
It takes three parameters: how (operation type), set (new mask), oldset (previous mask)
Returns 0 on success, -1 on failure

questions.question: Differentiate between SIG_BLOCK, SIG_UNBLOCK, and SIG_SETMASK operations in sigprocmask().
keyPoints: SIG_BLOCK: Adds signals to current mask (OR operation)
SIG_UNBLOCK: Removes signals from current mask
SIG_SETMASK: Replaces entire mask with new set
SIG_BLOCK preserves existing blocked signals; SIG_SETMASK replaces them completely

questions.question: Write the steps to block a specific signal (e.g., SIGINT) using sigprocmask().
keyPoints: Declare sigset_t variable for new set and old set
Initialize empty set using sigemptyset()
Add desired signal using sigaddset() (e.g., sigaddset(&newset, SIGINT))
Call sigprocmask(SIG_BLOCK, &newset, &oldset) to block the signal
Check return value for errors

questions.question: What is signal masking? Explain its significance in concurrent programming.
keyPoints: Signal masking is blocking certain signals from delivery
When masked, signals become pending instead of being delivered
Allows atomic execution of critical code sections
Prevents race conditions between signal handlers and main program flow
Critical for writing robust multi-threaded and event-driven programs

questions.question: Explain the relationship between sigprocmask(), signal handlers, and pending signals.
keyPoints: Signal handlers execute only when signals are not masked
Blocked signals remain pending in kernel's pending signal queue
When unmasked, pending signals are delivered immediately
sigprocmask() can protect code sections from signal interruption
Multiple occurrences of same signal may be merged to one pending signal

questions.question: What are the functions used to manipulate signal sets? Explain any two.
keyPoints: sigemptyset(): Initializes set to contain no signals
sigfillset(): Initializes set to contain all signals
sigaddset(): Adds a signal to set
sigdelset(): Removes a signal from set
sigismember(): Tests if signal is member of set

questions.question: Write a C program that demonstrates the use of sigprocmask() to block and unblock SIGUSR1 signal. Include proper error handling and explain the behavior of pending signals.
keyPoints: Define signal handler for SIGUSR1
Create sigset_t variables and initialize empty set
Block SIGUSR1 using sigprocmask(SIG_BLOCK)
Fork a child process or use alarm to send signal while blocked
Show signal remains pending
Unblock using sigprocmask(SIG_UNBLOCK)
Handler executes after unblocking
Include error checking for all function calls
Explain output demonstrating pending signal behavior

questions.question: Explain the concept of signal mask inheritance across fork() and exec() system calls. How does sigprocmask() behave in child processes?
keyPoints: After fork(), child inherits parent's signal mask
Blocked signals remain blocked in child process
Pending signals are cleared in child (only pending in parent)
After exec(), signal dispositions are reset to default
New program's signal handlers may differ
exec() preserves signal mask (blocked signals remain blocked)
sigprocmask() can modify mask in child independently
Explain practical implications for daemon processes

questions.question: Design a critical section protection mechanism using sigprocmask() for a multi-threaded application. Explain with code structure and discuss potential issues.
keyPoints: Use sigprocmask(SIG_BLOCK) at start of critical section
Block relevant signals before entering critical code
Use sigprocmask(SIG_SETMASK) or SIG_UNBLOCK to restore
Alternative: use pthread_sigmask() for thread-specific masking
Issue: nested critical sections can cause problems
Issue: must ensure proper restoration to avoid signal loss
Use sigaction() with SA_RESTART for reliable handler behavior
Discuss trade-offs between coarse and fine-grained signal blocking

questions.question: Compare sigprocmask() with pthread_sigmask(). Explain when and why you would use each in multi-threaded programs.
keyPoints: pthread_sigmask() works at thread level, affects calling thread only
sigprocmask() works at process level, affects all threads
pthread_sigmask() is preferred in multi-threaded programs
sigprocmask() affects entire process and all threads
Both have same interface (how, set, oldset)
Thread-specific blocking allows finer control
Process-level blocking useful for signal handling threads
Explain scenarios: dedicated signal handler thread vs. main thread handling
